Name: Ja'Marr Chase
Outlook: What a magical season it was for Chase in 2024 as he basically lapped the field in fantasy points. For context, Justin Jefferson was the WR2, and he scored 74 fewer points than Chase. 17 TDs and 1700 yards will do that! With this Cincy defense projected to struggle again and the Bengals a lock for a top-5 pass rate, Chase is once again an awesome pick in the first round of redraft leagues.

Name: CeeDee Lamb
Outlook: When you consider that Lamb played through a shoulder injury and lost Dak in Week 9, his 2024 season was actually pretty incredible. Despite those factors, Lamb still finished as the WR5 while averaging the 8th most yards in the league. With Dak now healthy and George Pickens in town to help take some attention off him, Lamb should go bonkers this year. He’s a fantastic target in Round 1 of your drafts.

Name: Justin Jefferson
Outlook: The only WR we might take over Jefferson in redraft this year is Ja’Marr Chase, and honestly, if you prefer JJetts, that’s cool, too. Excluding 2023 when he only played 10 games because of injury, he’s finished as a top-6 fantasy WR in every season of his career while averaging just under 1,600 yards in those seasons. He’s a pretty easy click in the first half of Round 1 this year.

Name: Amon-Ra St. Brown
Outlook: There is some slight concern that the Detroit offense isn’t quite as efficient without Ben Johnson in town, but that could affect more of the secondary pieces in Detroit’s offense. Amon-Ra will still get his as Jared Goff’s go-to guy. With 100+ catches and 1,100+ yards in three straight seasons, St. Brown is one of the most bankable WRs in fantasy. For those with early drafts, we recommend keeping a close eye on St. Brown throughout the summer as he revealed in early June that he hasn't participated in Detroit's off-season program after recovering from a knee scope. We expect him to be healthy for Week 1, but he's definitely a guy to monitor once training camp begins.

Name: Puka Nacua
Outlook: Remember Puka’s 2024 start to the season? The PCL injury in camp lingered for the first two months, then BAM. He’s back and he’s dominating. From Weeks 8-17, Puka averaged over 10 targets per game for a 33% market share. In that sample, only Ja’Marr Chase had more targets, catches, and yards. Even if Davante Adams adds more target competition than Cooper Kupp, this offense is so condensed that both guys can push for 25+% target shares and be awesome for fantasy. Puka is a great target in the first round of drafts.

Name: Malik Nabers
Outlook: When you finish as the WR7 while catching passes from Daniel Jones, Drew Lock, Tim Boyle, and Tommy Cutlets 🤌, you are #Good at the game. Yep, Nabers is legit! He delivered on the promise in a huge way while being a target earning machine. The 2024 sixth overall pick saw an average of 11.3 targets per game for a 34.9% market share. For context on those numbers, Ja’Marr Chase (10.3), CeeDee Lamb (10.1), and Puka Nacua (9.6) all saw fewer targets per game than Nabers. The QB play is once again going to be a headache, and the Giants probably won’t score a ton of TDs, but his share of the opportunities and talent profile is so good that it can offset some of those concerns.

Name: Nico Collins
Outlook: Collins missed some time last year with a hamstring injury, but when he was on the field, it was business as usual for Stroud’s WR1. Despite playing in just 12 games, he still went over 1,000 yards and was on pace for over 1,400 and 10 TDs. If Stroud can bounce back a bit to what we saw during his rookie season, Collins is on the short list of guys capable of finishing as the WR1 overall in fantasy.

Name: Brian Thomas Jr.
Outlook: BTJ was everything as a rookie. The superstar (yes, we’re going there already) averaged 14.1 fantasy points per game and finished as fantasy’s WR4 despite catching passes from McCorkle Jones for half the year. Travis Hunter will provide some increased target competition, but generally speaking, we’re not worried about that at all, as the offense in general should be far more efficient with this new staff. If Liam Cohen can get the best from Trevor Lawrence, Thomas is one of just a handful of WRs capable of finishing as the top scorer at his position.

Name: Drake London
Outlook: London put it all together in 2024 with the new coaching staff that actually prioritized throwing the football (C’mon Arthur!) Finishing as the WR6 on the back of 100 catches and nine scores, London dominated in the red zone. His 25 targets inside the 20-yard line were tied for the fourth most in the league. If Penix can take a step forward in his first full season as the starter, London should once again have a fantastic season as a top-15 option.

Name: Ladd McConkey
Outlook: McConkey is the classic Year 2 profile we like betting on in fantasy. He was a highly touted prospect coming out of Georgia; he produced in Year 1, and he’s attached to a great QB who can help Ladd further develop in the NFL. We know G Ro and HarDog want to establish it, and after signing Najee and drafting Omarion Hampton in Round 1, that should continue, but when you are pushing for a 25-30% target share, some of those concerns are mitigated. Expect Ladd to keep it rolling out of the slot as a high floor, high ceiling pick in fantasy.

Name: A.J. Brown
Outlook: The Eagles' pass game last year took a step back with Saquon Barkley doing some historic things. As a result, we saw Brown’s targets per game drop from 9.3 in 2023 to 7.5 in 2024. It doesn’t sound like a lot, but across the entire season, that adds up pretty quickly. Philly was one of the most run-heavy teams in the NFL last year, and if Saquon is healthy, that could certainly continue. One thing to consider, however, is that the Eagles’ schedule is much more difficult this season, and they lost some key pieces defensively. If they regress on that side of the ball, Jalen Hurts will be forced to air it out way more than he did in 2024. If he does, Brown should be able to bounce back as a top-12 guy. The talent is undeniable.

Name: Tee Higgins
Outlook: Higgins is coming off a career season in 2024, and he was rewarded with a big bag of money. We know Chase is the alpha here, but Tee is still seeing a 20%+ target share year over year, even with Chase doing Chase things. Considering how much this offense throws the football, that’s plenty of volume for Tee to continue to post top-20 numbers. If Chase happens to miss time, you’ve also got massive contingent upside with a guy who will be ranked as a top-12 WR in those weeks. He’s a perfect WR2 for your squad.

Name: Marvin Harrison Jr.
Outlook: The hype just got way too out of control last year. A consensus second round pick in redraft, the expectations were just too high for the former 4th overall pick in the Draft. We’re not overreacting to Harrison’s rookie season, which we’d classify as “good, not great.” This is still a talented player, and we generally see these types of guys make a jump in Year 2. Now that his ADP is more palatable, we’re fine taking shots on him as your WR2.

Name: Mike Evans
Outlook: Death, taxes, and Mike Evans 1,000-yard seasons. The future Hall of Famer once again crossed the 1,000-yard mark in 2024 for the 11th season in a row. Granted, it did take the final seconds of that Week 18 game, but who cares! Evans has been a top-15 fantasy WR year in and year out, and the peripheral metrics say he is still a great player. Over the last two years, Evans has averaged 2.33 and 2.52 yards per route run, which are some of the best marks in all of football. Father time does come for us all (ask Andy), but all signs suggest that Evans is still an efficient player, even at age 32. We do have some concerns about Baker Mayfield’s TD regression, so maybe that 11-14 TD season isn’t likely, but 8-10 scores and 1,000 yards is still doable for one of the best WRs over the last decade.
